g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Declaring Structures in C. Let's explore how to create powerful user-defined data types.
A structure is a user-defined data type that groups related data items of different types together under one name.
Structures let us model real-world entities like students, employees, and game objects with multiple properties.
g.text:
Student: name, id, gpa
Employee: name, salary, dept
Why Structures? • Group related data together • Create complex data models • Foundation for linked lists

g.data-narration: The basic syntax uses the struct keyword followed by a tag name and braces containing member declarations.
The structure tag is an identifier used to declare variables of this type later. It's optional but recommended.
g.text: The tag name identifies the structure type. Use it to create variables:

g.data-narration: Members are the data items inside a structure. They can be any valid C data type including arrays and pointers.
g.text: Members can be any valid C data type:

g.data-narration: Once a structure is defined, create variables using the struct keyword followed by the tag name.
g.text: struct Student s1, s2; // Two variables

g.data-narration: Use the dot operator to access individual members of a structure variable. The syntax is variable.member.
g.text: Use the dot (.) operator to access or modify individual members:

g.data-narration: Use typedef to create an alias, eliminating the need to type 'struct' keyword every time you declare a variable.
g.text: Create an alias to avoid typing "struct" every time:

g.data-narration: Self-referential structures contain a pointer to the same structure type, essential for linked lists and trees.
g.text: Contains a pointer to the SAME structure type - used for linked lists:

g.data-narration: Anonymous structures have no tag name and are useful when used with typedef for one-time type definitions.
g.text: Structures without a tag name - useful with typedef for single-use types:
typedef struct { int x, y; } Point;

g.data-narration: Initialize structure variables at declaration using curly braces with comma-separated values in member order.
g.text: Initialize at declaration using curly braces:

g.data-narration: Here's a complete example showing a Student structure with multiple member types and how to use it.
Structure declaration creates a blueprint - no memory is allocated until you declare actual variables.
Key takeaways: Use struct keyword for declaration, dot operator for member access, and typedef for convenience.
This concludes our lecture on declaring structures. Practice creating structures with various member types to master this fundamental concept.
g.text: Now you can create powerful user-defined data types in C!
